summary:
The French-American Alliance (February 6, 1778 AD) was the military and commercial treaty between France and the United States that transformed the [[event:106|American Revolutionary War]] from a colonial rebellion into a global conflict — France's entry bringing naval power, financial subsidies, and military expertise that would prove decisive at [[event:2352|Yorktown]] and in securing American independence. France had been secretly supplying the Americans since 1776 through the fictional trading company Rodrigue Hortalez et Cie, organized by the playwright and secret agent Pierre-Augustin Caron de Beaumarchais; the [[event:2395|American victory at Saratoga]] (October 1777) convinced the French foreign minister Vergennes that American independence was achievable and that public alliance was worth the risk of war with Britain.

The French-American Alliance was both an act of Enlightenment sympathy — many French officers, including the Marquis de Lafayette, were genuinely inspired by American republicanism — and a calculated act of revenge for the [[event:2332|Seven Years' War]] humiliation, in which France had lost most of its colonial empire to Britain. France hoped not only to weaken Britain but to recover lost territories; the [[event:2477|Treaty of Paris]] (1783) gave France minor territorial gains but not its major losses. The alliance created the world's first formally recognized American diplomatic relationships: Benjamin Franklin's courtly success in Paris, where he cultivated French public opinion and negotiated with Vergennes, became one of history's most celebrated diplomatic achievements. The cost of the American war would contribute significantly to France's fiscal crisis that led to the [[event:107|French Revolution]] six years after American independence was secured.
